medications.indication_rationale: An innocent murmur requires no medication. The management is reassurance, an in-person examination to characterise the murmur, and no activity restriction. There is no drug to prescribe and, as a paediatric case, no dosing is calculated.

Endocarditis prophylaxis must NOT be recommended for a murmur. Innocent murmurs carry no endocarditis risk and current guidance does not support prophylaxis here — recommending it is a dangerous error of commission and unnecessary antibiotic exposure.

medications.drug_name: Sedative or anxiolytic (for the child or the anxious parent)
medications.indication_rationale: No sedative is indicated. Parental anxiety is managed with clear reassurance and safety-netting, not medication; the child is well and needs nothing.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Encourage the child to continue full, unrestricted participation in all sport and play, including competitive soccer.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: No activity restriction is warranted for an innocent murmur; normal vigorous activity is safe and demonstrates a healthy heart.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Coach the parents to treat the child entirely normally — allow normal sleep and play, and avoid hovering or repeatedly asking how he feels.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Normalising the child's routine dismantles the parents' cycle of hypervigilance; watching him thrive is itself reassuring evidence the heart is healthy.

patient_education_points.point: A murmur is simply the sound of blood flowing through the heart — like water through a pipe. In slim, active children with a vigorous heartbeat this normal sound is often heard; it does not mean the heart is damaged.
Because this is a telehealth visit I cannot listen to his chest myself, so I cannot confirm the type of murmur over the phone — that is why an in-person examination is needed to check it properly.
He does not need to go to the emergency department tonight and does not need an ambulance — he is well, active and has no warning signs. A routine in-person check is the right next step.
Innocent murmurs are very common in healthy children and usually become inaudible as the child grows.

follow_up_plan.interval: in-person GP (or paediatric) review within 1–2 weeks
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Auscultation and characterisation of the murmur, pulses and blood pressure, exertional symptom screen and growth; arrange echocardiography only if features are atypical or for definitive reassurance
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any fainting, exertional chest pain or breathlessness, blue lips, poor feeding or sudden extreme lethargy — seek urgent/emergency care rather than waiting for the routine review

must_include_items: Recognise this as a newly-detected, asymptomatic murmur in a well child and acknowledge the AI cannot auscultate over telehealth
Actively screen for red flags — exertional syncope, chest pain, breathlessness, infancy cyanosis and family history of sudden cardiac death
Reassure the parents that emergency department attendance tonight and 000 are NOT required for an asymptomatic child with no red flags
Arrange an in-person GP or paediatric examination within 1–2 weeks to characterise the murmur
Provide clear safety-netting for the red flags that would warrant urgent or emergency review
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending a prompt (24–48 h) in-person GP review instead of 1–2 weeks is acceptable if the clinician judges the parents need earlier hands-on reassurance — this is mild, defensible over-triage, not an error.
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for exertional syncope, chest pain or family history of sudden death
Failing to arrange any in-person examination of the murmur
Failing to give red-flag safety-netting advice
errors_of_commission: Declaring the murmur 'innocent/benign' remotely without in-person auscultation
Over-triaging an asymptomatic well child to the emergency department overnight or to 000
Prescribing a sedative or any medication
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES both (a) resisting inappropriate over-triage — no ED tonight / no 000 for this asymptomatic child — AND (b) arranging an appropriate in-person examination with red-flag safety-netting. Declaring the murmur innocent remotely, or over-triaging to the ED/000, is a failure regardless of other content.
